schools.summary: Gill: this is a transition to another part of the epistle; the apostle, having largely described the false teachers, the secret enemies of the Christian religion under a profession of it, passes on to take notice of the more open adversaries and profane scoffers of it; Peter declaring that this is the second epistle he writes, in both stirring up their pure minds by way of remembrance — the apostle's aim to awaken the saints' memory of revealed truth (Peter, opening the last part of his letter, telling his loving purpose: 'this second epistle, beloved, I now write unto you,' a second letter to the same beloved saints, 'in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance'; the gracious method, that Peter does not assume the saints are ignorant but 'stir[s] up' their 'pure minds,' sincere uncorrupted understandings, 'by way of remembrance,' recalling to their memory the truths they already knew, so even established believers need their minds awakened and stirred to keep firm hold of the doctrines they have received).

themes: this second epistle, beloved, I now write unto you
in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance
the stirring up of the saints by remembrance
even established believers needing their minds awakened

schools.summary: Barnes: 'that ye may be mindful of the words' — of the doctrines, the truths, the prophetic statements; Jude (verse 18) says that it had been foretold by the apostles that there would be such scoffers; Peter naming the end of his writing, that they be mindful of the words of the holy prophets and the commandment of the apostles — the joining of prophets and apostles as the twofold authority to be remembered (Peter naming the great body of truth he would have them keep in mind: 'the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets,' the Old Testament Scriptures and prophecies, 'and of the commandment of us the apostles of the Lord and Saviour,' the New Testament teaching of the apostles, Christ's own sent ones; the joining of these two, the prophets and the apostles, the Old and New Testament witness, set side by side as one harmonious authority, so the believer's stability lies in holding fast both the ancient prophets' words and the apostles' commandment, which together make up the revealed truth of God).

themes: the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets
and of the commandment of us the apostles of the Lord and Saviour
the twofold authority of prophets and apostles
the Old and New Testament witness as one

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: to quicken and excite us to a serious minding and firm adhering to what God has revealed to us by the prophets and apostles, we are told that there will be scoffers; Gill: this might easily be known and observed from the writings of the apostles and prophets; Peter declaring that, as a first thing to be known, there shall come in the last days scoffers walking after their own lusts — the foretelling of last-days scoffers driven by their own lusts (Peter, that the saints be forearmed, foretelling what they must expect: 'knowing this first,' as a chief and certain thing, 'that there shall come in the last days scoffers,' mockers who deride the faith and especially the hope of Christ's coming, 'walking after their own lusts'; the root of their scoffing, that these mockers are driven not by honest reason but by 'their own lusts,' the sinful desires they will not give up, for it is the love of sin that makes men scoff at the truth and the judgment to come, that they may sin without fear, so the rise of such scoffers is no surprise but a thing foretold, and their mockery springs from their own corruption).

themes: knowing this first, that there shall come in the last days scoffers
walking after their own lusts
the foretold coming of last-days scoffers
mockery springing from the love of sin

schools.summary: Gill: the object of their scorn and derision is the coming of the Lord and Saviour, whom they name not through contempt; the meaning is, what is become of the promise of his coming, where is the accomplishment of it? The prophets foretold he would come, and he himself said he would come; Peter reporting the scoffers' taunt — 'Where is the promise of his coming? since the fathers fell asleep, all things continue as they were' — the scoffers' argument from the seeming delay and constancy of nature (Peter setting out the very words of the mockers' scoff: 'Where is the promise of his coming?' a sneer at the long delay, where is this coming so often promised yet not come, 'for since the fathers fell asleep,' since the first generations died, 'all things continue as they were from the beginning of the creation'; the substance of their reasoning, that they argue from the apparent unbroken constancy of the natural order, the world going on as ever and nothing changing, that no great divine intervention or coming in judgment is to be expected, so the scoffers deny Christ's coming on the ground that the course of nature has never been interrupted and never will be).

themes: and saying, Where is the promise of his coming?
since the fathers fell asleep, all things continue as they were
the scoffers' argument from nature's constancy
denying Christ's coming because nature seems unbroken

schools.summary: Gill: these men, as had professed Christianity and had the advantage of revelation and the opportunity of reading the Scriptures, might have known that the heavens and the earth were from the beginning, that they were made by the word of God, and that the earth was formed out of and amid the waters; their ignorance was wilful; Peter declaring that the scoffers willingly are ignorant that by the word of God the heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of and in the water — the wilful ignorance that forgets creation by God's word (Peter answering the scoffers by exposing their wilful blindness to a great fact: 'for this they willingly are ignorant of,' it being no honest mistake but a deliberate shutting of their eyes, namely 'that by the word of God the heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of the water and in the water'; the point of the argument, that the same God who by his bare word made the heavens and formed the earth out of and amid the waters is no idle spectator bound by nature's fixed course, the scoffers conveniently forgetting that the world they call unchangeable was itself called into being by God's word and so is wholly at his command).

themes: for this they willingly are ignorant of
that by the word of God the heavens were of old
the earth standing out of the water and in the water
wilful ignorance forgetting creation by God's word

schools.summary: Gill: 'the world that then was' is the old world, the world before the flood, which had stood from the creation 1656 years; this world, being overflowed with water, perished; Peter declaring that the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished — the flood as proof that the world's order has indeed been broken by God (Peter driving home the answer to the scoffers' claim that 'all things continue as they were': they are wrong, for once already God broke the course of nature in judgment, 'whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished'; the force, that by the same 'word of God' and the same waters out of which the earth was formed, the old world, the world of Noah's day, was destroyed in the great flood, so the scoffers' premise that nature's course has never been interrupted is false, for God did once judge the world by water and the very Scriptures the scoffers profess record it, so the flood stands as the standing witness that God can and does break in upon the world in judgment).

themes: whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water
perished
the flood as proof of God's judgment
the scoffers' premise of unbroken nature refuted

schools.summary: Gill: 'the heavens and the earth which are now' are in being, in distinction from and opposition to the heavens that were of old and the earth standing in and out of the water, and the world that then was when the waters of the flood overflowed it; Peter declaring that the present heavens and earth, by the same word, are kept in store, reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of the ungodly — the present world reserved by God's word for a coming judgment by fire (Peter completing the parallel with the flood: as the old world perished by water, so the present world is appointed to perish by fire, 'the heavens and the earth, which are now, by the same word,' the same word of God that made and once flooded the world, 'are kept in store, reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men'; the certainty and the design, that the world is not left to drift on for ever but is being 'kept in store,' reserved by God's word like fuel laid up, for a fixed 'day of judgment' when it shall be destroyed by fire and the ungodly perish, so the very constancy the scoffers trust in is but God's patient preserving of the world for the appointed day of fiery judgment).

themes: but the heavens and the earth, which are now, by the same word are kept in store
reserved unto fire against the day of judgment
and perdition of ungodly men
the present world kept for a fixed day of fiery judgment

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle addresses the saints he writes to, for whom he had a tender affection and was concerned, lest they be stumbled at the length of time since the promise of Christ's coming was given, which these scoffers object; Peter charging the beloved not to be ignorant that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day — the eternal God to whom no length of time is long or short (Peter, against the scoffers' taunt of delay, reminding the saints of God's relation to time: 'be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day'; the great truth, that God is eternal, dwelling above time, so that to him the longest span and the shortest are alike, what seems a long delay to short-lived men being nothing to the everlasting God, for whom a thousand years pass as a day and a single day holds the fulness of a thousand years, so the seeming slowness of Christ's coming is no real delay with God, who measures not by man's impatient clock but by his own eternal counsel).

themes: be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years
and a thousand years as one day
God's eternal relation to time
the seeming delay no real delay with the everlasting God

schools.summary: Gill: the words seem rather to regard the particular promise of Christ's coming; the Lord is not slack, as some men count slackness, but is longsuffering toward his elect (as 'to us-ward'), whom he bears with much and long before conversion, not willing that any of them should perish but that all of them should come to repentance (his longsuffering toward the wicked issuing not in their salvation but their sorer punishment); Peter declaring that the Lord is not slack concerning his promise but is longsuffering toward us, not willing that any should perish but that all should come to repentance — the longsuffering of God that delays judgment for the sake of repentance (Peter giving the true reason for the delay the scoffers mock: 'the Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness,' it being not negligence or failure to keep his word, 'but is longsuffering to us-ward, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance'; the Reformed taking 'any' and 'all' of God's elect, 'to us-ward,' whom God patiently waits to bring to repentance, so the delay is the very space of their salvation, while others read it of a more general sincere will that none perish but all repent, both agreeing the delay springs from God's saving longsuffering).

debates.question: Whom is God 'not willing that any should perish'?

sides.position: God's elect, 'to us-ward' — whom he patiently bears and waits to bring to repentance, so the delay is the very space of their salvation
All men generally — a sincere will that none perish but all come to repentance

themes: the Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness
but is longsuffering to us-ward
not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance
the delay the space of salvation

schools.summary: Gill: the Lord will come in that day which he has fixed, according to his promise, than which nothing is more certain, and he will come as a thief in the night, suddenly and unexpectedly (as his first coming was in the night, so perhaps his second); Peter declaring that the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night, in which the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, the elements melt with fervent heat, and the earth be burned up — the certain, sudden day of the Lord and the fiery dissolution of the world (Peter, having shown the delay to be longsuffering, affirming the certainty and manner of the end: 'but the day of the Lord will come,' it surely will come however delayed, 'as a thief in the night,' suddenly and unexpectedly when men look not for it, and painting the awful dissolution: 'the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up'; the scene of the great day, the whole frame of the visible universe consumed by fire, the heavens vanishing with a roar, the very elements melting, and the earth with all man's works burned up, so the world the scoffers thought unchangeable shall be utterly dissolved in fire at the sudden coming of the Lord).

themes: but the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night
the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat
the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up
the world utterly dissolved in fire at the sudden day

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the apostle, having instructed them in the doctrine of the last things, applies it; Gill: since nothing is more certain than the dissolution of all things by fire (the heavens with all their host, the earth and all that is upon it), this should engage to holiness; Peter asking, seeing all these things shall be dissolved, what manner of persons they ought to be in all holy conduct and godliness — the holy living that the certain dissolution of the world should produce (Peter, from the great fact of the coming dissolution, drawing the searching practical question: 'seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved,' since the whole visible world is destined to be melted and burned up, 'what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness'; the force of the appeal, that if all earthly things are so soon to pass away in fire, how loose should the believer sit to them and how earnestly should he give himself to 'holy conversation and godliness,' a life wholly set apart to God, so the doctrine of the end is no idle speculation but a powerful spur to holiness, the certainty that this world is passing making the Christian a holy, heavenly-minded man).

themes: seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved
what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness
the holy living the world's dissolution should produce
sitting loose to passing earthly things

schools.summary: Gill: 'the day of God' is the same with the day of the Lord, and so the Vulgate and Arabic read; it intends the day of Christ's second coming to judgment, and so is a proof of the deity of Christ, being called 'the day of God' in distinction from man's day or human judgment; Peter charging that they look for and hasten unto the coming of the day of God, in which the heavens shall be dissolved and the elements melt — the eager expectation and hastening of the day of God's coming (Peter setting the right posture of the holy life toward the end: 'looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God,' not dreading but eagerly expecting it, and 'hasting,' earnestly desiring it, longing for it, and hastening it by prayer and holy living, 'wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat'; the title 'the day of God,' for it is the day of Christ's coming, here called God's day, a testimony to Christ's deity, and the believer's stance being not the scoffer's mockery nor a fearful dread but a glad looking-for and a longing that hastens toward the day when the heavens shall be dissolved in fire).

debates.question: Why is the day of Christ's coming called 'the day of God'?

sides.position: It is a testimony to the deity of Christ — the day of his second coming to judgment is called God's day, marking him as God

themes: looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God
wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved
the eager looking for and hastening of the day of God
'the day of God' a testimony to Christ's deity

schools.summary: Barnes: the allusion here is, beyond a doubt, to two passages in Isaiah in which a promise of this kind is found — Isaiah 65:17, 'For, behold, I create new heavens and a new earth,' and Isaiah 66:22; Gill notes 'according to his promise,' or promises; Peter declaring that, according to God's promise, we look for new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells — the blessed hope beyond the fire, a new world where righteousness dwells (Peter, having shown the present world's dissolution, lifting the believer's eye past the fire to the glorious hope beyond: 'nevertheless we, according to his promise,' resting on God's own promise given by Isaiah, 'look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness'; the comfort, that the burning up of the old is not the end but the way to a new and better creation, the believer looking for 'new heavens and a new earth,' a renewed and glorified world, and its crowning glory being that 'righteousness' shall 'dwell' there, a world wholly cleansed of sin, the everlasting home of holiness, so beyond the ruin of the old world lies the believer's true and abiding hope, a new creation where righteousness is at home).

themes: nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth
wherein dwelleth righteousness
the hope beyond the fire of a new creation
a world cleansed of sin, the home of righteousness

schools.summary: Barnes: the effect of such hopes and prospects should be to lead us to be diligent in securing our salvation, to be found of him in peace; Gill notes 'seeing ye look for these things,' for the burning of the heavens and the earth, the coming of Christ, and the new heavens and new earth; Peter charging that, since they look for such things, they be diligent to be found of him in peace, without spot and blameless — the diligent pursuit of peace and purity in expectation of the day (Peter drawing the practical conclusion from the hope of the new creation: 'wherefore, beloved, seeing that ye look for such things,' since you expect the dissolution of the old and the coming of the new, 'be diligent,' give earnest all-out diligence, 'that ye may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless'; the threefold aim, to be found when Christ comes 'in peace,' reconciled to God and at rest with him, 'without spot,' pure and unstained by sin, and 'blameless,' against whom no charge can stand, so the believer's looking for the day is to make him earnest now to be ready for it, pure and at peace, that the great day may find him prepared).

themes: wherefore, beloved, seeing that ye look for such things, be diligent
that ye may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless
the diligent pursuit of peace and purity
the hope of the day making the believer ready for it

schools.summary: Gill: this is not his longsuffering toward the wicked, his forbearance with them (for that is not the means of, nor way to, nor does it issue in their salvation, but in their sorer punishment and greater damnation), but toward the elect, whom he bears much and long with before conversion; this longsuffering is salvation; Peter charging that they account the longsuffering of the Lord to be salvation, as Paul also wrote according to the wisdom given him — the Lord's patience reckoned as the very means of salvation, confirmed by Paul (Peter, returning to God's longsuffering, bidding them rightly interpret it: 'account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation,' reckon the Lord's patient delay not as slackness but as the very space and means of salvation, the time in which his people are brought to repentance, and confirming this by the witness of a fellow-apostle: 'even as our beloved brother Paul also according to the wisdom given unto him hath written unto you'; the warmth and weight, that Peter calls Paul 'our beloved brother,' owning him as a fellow-labourer who wrote 'according to the wisdom given unto him,' by divine inspiration, whose epistles taught the same truths, so the Lord's longsuffering is the season of salvation, as both Peter and Paul agree).

themes: and account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation
even as our beloved brother Paul also... hath written unto you
the Lord's patience reckoned as the means of salvation
confirmed by Paul, writing by the wisdom given him

schools.summary: Gill: from this it appears that the Apostle Paul had by this time written several of his epistles, if not all, written according to the same wisdom and under the influence of the same Spirit as his epistle to the Hebrews; Peter observing that in all Paul's epistles, speaking of these things, are some things hard to be understood, which the unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do the other Scriptures, to their own destruction — Paul's epistles owned as Scripture, and the peril of twisting the hard parts (Peter setting Paul's letters alongside the Old Testament as sacred Scripture and warning of their abuse: 'as also in all his epistles, speaking in them of these things,' Paul wrote of these same truths throughout his letters, 'in which are some things hard to be understood,' certain deep and difficult passages, 'which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do also the other scriptures, unto their own destruction'; two weighty things, first that Peter ranks 'all his epistles' with 'the other scriptures,' a clear witness that Paul's writings are inspired Scripture, and second the warning that the untaught and unsettled twist and distort the hard passages of Scripture, Paul's and others', to their own ruin, so the difficulty lies not in the word but in the corrupt handling of it by the unstable).

debates.question: What does Peter's phrase 'as they do also the other scriptures' imply about Paul's epistles?

sides.position: That Paul's epistles are themselves inspired Scripture — Peter ranks 'all his epistles' alongside 'the other scriptures,' a clear apostolic witness to their canonical authority

themes: as also in all his epistles, speaking in them of these things
in which are some things hard to be understood
which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest... unto their own destruction
Paul's epistles ranked with Scripture

schools.summary: Barnes: being aware of this danger and knowing that such results would follow, beware; Gill: they knew beforehand that there would be such mockers and scoffers in the last days, and such unlearned and unstable men that would deprave the Scriptures and wrest them; Peter warning the beloved, since they know these things beforehand, to beware lest, led away by the error of the wicked, they fall from their steadfastness — the warning to the forewarned not to be carried away from steadfastness by error (Peter applying the whole to his readers' watchfulness: 'ye therefore, beloved, seeing ye know these things before,' being forewarned of the scoffers and the wresting of Scripture, 'beware,' be on your guard, 'lest ye also, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness'; the peril and the safeguard, that the danger is real, that even the forewarned may be 'led away' by the plausible 'error of the wicked' and so 'fall from' their 'own stedfastness,' their firm standing in the faith, so knowledge of the danger is given precisely that they may watch and not be swept from their settled footing in the truth).

themes: ye therefore, beloved, seeing ye know these things before, beware
lest ye also, being led away with the error of the wicked
fall from your own stedfastness
forewarned, that they may watch and not fall

schools.summary: Gill: 'grow in grace,' in the gifts of grace, which under a divine blessing may be increased by using them, for gifts neglected decrease but stirred up and used are improved and increase, and though men are to be thankful for their gifts and content, they may lawfully desire more and seek an increase; Peter charging that they grow in grace and in the knowledge of Christ, and closing with the doxology, 'To him be glory both now and for ever. Amen' — the positive safeguard of growth in grace, sealed with glory to Christ (Peter ending the epistle with the great preservative against falling: not standing still but going forward, 'but grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ'; the twofold growth, in 'grace,' in faith, love, holiness, and all the graces of the Spirit, and in 'the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ,' an ever-deepening acquaintance with Christ himself, the surest guard against being led away by error, and crowning the whole letter with a doxology to Christ: 'to him be glory both now and for ever. Amen,' ascribing to the Lord and Saviour eternal glory, a fitting witness to his deity).

debates.question: What does the doxology 'to him be glory both now and for ever' testify of Christ?

sides.position: His deity — ascribing eternal glory to the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ is a fitting witness that he is God

themes: but grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ
to him be glory both now and for ever. Amen
growth in grace the surest guard against error
the doxology to Christ a witness to his deity
